ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn entry exists specifically for Chinese Sausage Fried Carrot Cake (chai tow kway with lap cheong). Closest defensible FoodOn ancestor is fried rice cake food product (FOODON_03315595); the Chinese sausage and egg components are not captured in this code.
ontology.foodex2_description: Stir-fried mixed dish — rice cake base with meat and egg
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (mixed stir-fried dish) is the closest defensible category. No exact FoodEx2 entry exists for chai tow kway with Chinese sausage. Closest parent match documented.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical food concept exists for this specific Singaporean hawker dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard hawker plate of Chinese Sausage Fried Carrot Cake weighs approximately 400 g and constitutes a complete single-person meal serving.

nutrient_source.title: Health Promotion Board Singapore — Nutrient Composition of Food (HPB Foodo / Singapore Food Composition Database)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single HPB entry exists for Chinese Sausage Fried Carrot Cake with lap cheong. Nutrient values are derived by component-weighted estimation using HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entries for chai tow kway (fried carrot cake, dark), Chinese sausage (lap cheong), egg (fried), and preserved radish (chai poh), cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for analogous ingredients. Input record values (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were reviewed against this composite estimate and retained where consistent. Confidence is set to medium.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; University of Sydney GI Database — Rice cake / rice flour-based products
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Chinese Sausage Fried Carrot Cake. GI of 68 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for rice cake / rice flour-based steamed and fried products (GI range 64–72, mean ~68), which is the primary carbohydrate base of this dish (radish cake made from rice flour). The fat and protein from Chinese sausage and egg will modestly attenuate the glycaemic response in practice. Glycaemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (68 × 40.0) / 100 = 27.2 for a 400 g plate (10.0 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 4 = 40.0 g carbohydrate per serving).

hypertension.key_factor: High cumulative sodium load from multiple processed ingredients: dark soy sauce, light soy sauce, fish sauce, preserved radish (chai poh), and Chinese sausage (lap cheong) collectively deliver 375 mg sodium per 100 g, equating to approximately 1,500 mg per standard 400 g plate — 65–75% of the recommended daily sodium limit for hypertensive individuals.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit this dish to a maximum of 100 g per occasion, request reduced soy sauce and no fish sauce, and avoid adding chilli paste or additional condiments; ideally this dish should be avoided on days when other high-sodium foods are consumed.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Rice flour-based radish cake has a medium-to-high glycaemic index (~68) with low dietary fibre (0.75 g/100 g), resulting in a high glycaemic load of 27.2 per standard 400 g plate; Chinese sausage contributes added sugars that further elevate postprandial glucose response.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should restrict portion size to no more than 150–200 g, pair with non-starchy vegetables to increase fibre, request preparation with less soy sauce and no added sugar, and monitor postprandial blood glucose closely given the high glycaemic load per serving.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 3.75 g/100 g (15.0 g per 400 g plate) from Chinese sausage and cooking oil, combined with cholesterol at 62.5 mg/100 g (250 mg per plate) and high sodium (1,500 mg per plate), creates a triple cardiovascular burden of atherogenic lipids, dietary cholesterol, and sodium-driven blood pressure elevation.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should avoid or strictly limit this dish due to its high saturated fat, cholesterol, and sodium content; if consumed, portion should not exceed 100 g and frequency should be no more than once per fortnight as part of an otherwise heart-healthy dietary pattern.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Chinese sausage (lap cheong) is a processed pork product rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6) and saturated fat, amplifying pro-inflammatory eicosanoid pathways; high sodium from multiple condiments worsens tissue oedema and joint swelling.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish as it contains processed red meat with high arachidonic acid content and multiple high-sodium condiments, with no meaningful anti-inflammatory nutrients to offset the inflammatory burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chinese Sausage Fried Carrot Cake (腊肠菜頭粿; Lap Cheong Chai Tow Kway) is a popular Singaporean hawker dish that combines stir-fried radish cake cubes with sliced Chinese sausage (lap cheong), scrambled or folded egg, preserved salted radish (chai poh), and a savoury dark soy sauce mixture cooked in a hot wok. The radish cake (chai tow kway) is made from steamed rice flour and grated white radish, then cut into cubes and wok-fried until the exterior is lightly caramelised and crisp while the interior remains soft and chewy. Chinese sausage (lap cheong) is a sweet-savoury cured pork sausage that adds richness, umami depth, and a slightly sweet note. The dish is typically finished with dark soy sauce for colour and flavour, and chai poh (preserved radish) for a salty, crunchy contrast. Nutritionally, a standard 400 g plate delivers approximately 650 kcal, 25 g protein, 45 g fat, and 40 g carbohydrate, with 1,500 mg sodium — making it a calorie-dense, high-sodium meal. The rice flour base has a medium glycaemic index (~68) and a high glycaemic load per serving (~27), relevant for diabetes management. Saturated fat at 15 g per plate and cholesterol at 250 mg per plate are clinically significant for cardiovascular and lipid management. This dish is not suitable for hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or cardiovascular disease without significant portion restriction and ingredient modification. It is a staple of Singaporean hawker centres and coffee shops, commonly eaten as breakfast or lunch, and is culturally associated with Teochew and Hokkien Chinese culinary traditions transplanted to Singapore.

confidence_reason: No single approved source entry exists for Chinese Sausage Fried Carrot Cake with lap cheong. Nutrient values are derived from a component-weighted composite estimate using HPB Singapore Food Composition Database and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for constituent ingredients. The input record values (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were reviewed and retained where consistent with the composite estimate. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database rice flour-based product data. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for this composite stir-fried hawker dish are not available in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2024. potassium_mg set to -1: potassium data for this specific dish combination are not reported in the HPB database; estimated ~120 mg/100 g from ingredient analogy but not confirmed by an approved source and therefore set to -1 per numeric hygiene rules. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this composite dish are not available in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database or USDA FoodData Central for this specific preparation as of 2024. trans_fat_g of 0.13 g/100 g is retained from input record; this is plausible given the use of cooking oil and processed sausage in stir-frying. All core macros (protein_g 6.25, fat_g 11.25, carbohydrate_g 10.0) are non-zero and consistent with the composite ingredient estimate; no zero-macro justification is required. GI of 68 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database rice flour-based fried product data; direct GI measurement for this dish has not been published. Halal status is false as Chinese sausage (lap cheong) is a pork-based product.
